content:
9 min read
Just now

--

A technical breakdown of what NIM is, how it works internally, and what it takes to deploy and operate it — with real output from an RTX 5090.

If you have been running language models locally with Ollama, you already understand the basics. Pull a model, expose an endpoint, start sending requests.
What that setup does not give you is any of the things that matter when more than one person is using it: throughput visibility, KV cache control, a validated inference engine, or anything to look at when a request takes eight seconds and you do not know why.
NVIDIA NIM answers a specific question: what does it actually take to run a language model the same way you would run any other production service?

What NIM is
NIM — NVIDIA Inference Microservice — is a Docker container that bundles everything needed to serve a language model in production into a single, self-configuring unit.
When you run a NIM container, the following happens automatically:
docker run ...
↓
NIM inspects local GPU hardware
↓
Selects the optimal model profile for that hardware
↓
Downloads the model from NGC (or uses local cache)
↓
Launches the inference engine with validated configuration
↓
Exposes OpenAI-compatible API on port 8000Each step is handled by NIM. You do not choose an inference engine, configure quantization, or write a serving script. NIM makes those decisions based on what your hardware can actually support and what will perform best on it.
Inside the container:
Model weights ← downloaded from NGC, optimized by NVIDIA
Inference engine ← vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, or SGLang (profile-dependent)
API server ← OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions
Health probes ← /v1/health/live and /v1/health/ready
Prometheus metrics ← /metrics endpointThese are not optional extras. They are part of every NIM deployment.

Model profiles and hardware-aware selection
The concept that makes NIM different from simply running vLLM yourself is the model profile system.
A profile is a validated combination of:

• Model precision — fp8, fp16, bf16, or quantized variants

• Inference backend — vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, or SGLang

• Parallelism configuration — how many GPUs, tensor parallel degree, pipeline parallel degree
NIM maintains a manifest of profiles for each model. At startup, it inspects the available GPU hardware and selects the profile that is both compatible with the hardware and optimal for performance.
On high-end consumer GPUs such as the RTX 5090, NIM selects a vLLM-based fp8 profile. The startup log confirms the selection:
Starting vLLM v0.22.1
Model: meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct
Precision: fp8
TP/PP: 1/1
CPU: 3 cores
Port: 8000
OpenAPI: http://localhost:8000/docs (interactive)Precision: fp8 means NVIDIA's own quantization format — not the community 4-bit GGUF files that Ollama uses. fp8 delivers better throughput than fp16 while maintaining quality that community quantization methods typically cannot match at the same compression ratio.
TP/PP: 1/1 is tensor parallelism 1 and pipeline parallelism 1 — single GPU, single stage. On multi-GPU systems, NIM automatically increases tensor parallelism to distribute model weights across available GPUs.
For GPU models where pre-built TRT-LLM engines exist, NIM downloads the optimized engine directly. For all other GPUs, it uses the vLLM backend with appropriate configuration. Either path requires no manual selection from the operator.

The architecture difference from Ollama
Ollama’s inference stack:
Consumer application
↓
Ollama HTTP API
↓
llama.cpp
↓
GPUNIM’s inference stack:
Consumer application
↓
NIM proxy (nginx) — liveness, readiness, TLS, CORS
↓
vLLM
↓
GPUThe difference is not cosmetic. vLLM implements continuous batching — a scheduling technique that packs multiple concurrent requests into the same GPU forward pass. Instead of processing requests one at a time, the inference engine dynamically fills available KV cache capacity with tokens from different in-flight requests.
Under a single user, both stacks feel similar. Under concurrent load, they behave completely differently. Ollama with llama.cpp processes requests sequentially by default. vLLM with continuous batching maintains near-constant GPU utilization regardless of how requests arrive.
The NIM proxy layer adds production-grade concerns that vLLM does not provide directly: separate liveness and readiness probes (so orchestrators can distinguish “container is running” from “model is loaded and ready”), TLS termination, CORS configuration, and fail-fast supervision. If either the proxy or the inference engine exits unexpectedly, the container shuts down cleanly so the orchestrator can reschedule it.

Prerequisites
NVIDIA Container Toolkit — NIM containers require GPU passthrough. If it is not already installed:
sudo apt install nvidia-container-toolkit -y
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart dockerVerify GPU access from within a container before proceeding:
docker run --rm --runtime=nvidia --gpus all ubuntu nvidia-smiNGC Personal API Key — NIM images are hosted on NVIDIA’s container registry (nvcr.io) and require authentication. Navigate to org.ngc.nvidia.com/setup/api-keys and generate a key with at minimum NGC Catalog access. Legacy API keys are not supported — only Personal API Keys work with NIM.
export NGC_API_KEY=nvapi-...docker login nvcr.io
# Username: $oauthtoken ← literal string, not your username
# Password: <your NGC_API_KEY>The username is always the literal string $oauthtoken for all users. This is NVIDIA's fixed credential format for registry authentication.

Deploying the container
docker run -it --rm \
--gpus all \
--shm-size=16GB \
-e NGC_API_KEY=$NGC_API_KEY \
-p 8000:8000 \
nvcr.io/nim/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct:latest--gpus all passes all available GPUs to the container. NIM requires direct GPU access. This flag is not optional.
--shm-size=16GB allocates shared memory for inter-process communication inside the container. vLLM uses /dev/shm to pass tensors between the API server process and the EngineCore process. Docker's default is 64MB — this is far too small for any LLM workload. For an 8B model, 8GB is sufficient. 16GB is a safe default that accommodates larger models without configuration changes.
On first run, NIM downloads the model from NGC. For llama-3.1–8b-instruct this is approximately 16GB. Subsequent runs use the cached model. To persist the cache across container restarts:
mkdir -p ~/.nim/cachedocker run -it --rm \
--gpus all \
--shm-size=16GB \
-e NGC_API_KEY=$NGC_API_KEY \
-v ~/.nim/cache:/opt/nim/.cache \
-p 8000:8000 \
nvcr.io/nim/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct:latest/opt/nim/.cache inside the container is where NIM stores downloaded model files. Mounting a local directory here means the model is only downloaded once regardless of how many times the container is restarted.

Verifying the endpoint
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{
"model": "meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct",
"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is KV cache?"}]
}'The response includes fields that are absent from Ollama responses:
{
"model": "meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct",
"system_fingerprint": "vllm-0.22.1-46d20459",
"usage": {
"prompt_tokens": 38,
"completion_tokens": 13,
"total_tokens": 51
}
}system_fingerprint identifies the exact vLLM build. In a production environment, tracking this across deployments is relevant for understanding performance changes between NIM versions.
usage reports token counts per request. Combined with LiteLLM, this enables per-team and per-model cost accounting even on self-hosted hardware — the same accountability as a paid external API.

GPU memory allocation and KV cache
On first run with default settings, nvidia-smi shows the container consuming nearly all available VRAM:
| 0 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
| 0% 35C P8 23W / 575W | 29699MiB / 32607MiB |This is intentional. vLLM’s default gpu_memory_utilization is 0.9 — it allocates 90% of available VRAM for the KV cache. A large KV cache means the engine can hold more concurrent in-flight requests before needing to evict and reload.
What KV cache is
Every transformer model generates key and value tensors during inference. For a request that produces 1000 output tokens, the model generates these tensors for each token in the sequence — and each new token requires access to all previous key-value pairs. KV cache stores these tensors in GPU memory so they do not need to be recomputed on each forward pass.
The memory cost of KV cache scales linearly with context length. This is why the default context window of 131072 tokens for llama-3.1–8b-instruct consumes nearly all available VRAM — the KV cache budget for a single maximum-length request is 16GB.
Controlling VRAM usage
NIM exposes vLLM configuration through NIM_PASSTHROUGH_ARGS:
docker run -it --rm \
--gpus all \
--shm-size=16GB \
-e NGC_API_KEY=$NGC_API_KEY \
-e NIM_PASSTHROUGH_ARGS="--gpu-memory-utilization 0.5 --max-model-len 8192" \
-p 8000:8000 \
nvcr.io/nim/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct:latest--gpu-memory-utilization 0.5 caps VRAM usage at 50% — approximately 16GB on a 32GB card.
--max-model-len 8192 sets the maximum context window to 8192 tokens. For most production use cases — chat assistants, internal tools, API consumers — 8192 tokens is more than sufficient. The reduction from 131072 to 8192 cuts KV cache memory requirements by approximately 94%.
After applying both flags:
| 0 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
| 0% 33C P8 22W / 575W | 16091MiB / 32607MiB |16GB instead of 30GB — the same model, the same inference quality for any conversation under 8192 tokens, half the VRAM. The remaining 16GB is available for other workloads on the same GPU.

The Prometheus metrics endpoint
Every NIM container exposes metrics at /metrics:
curl http://localhost:8000/metrics | grep -E "^vllm"vllm:num_requests_running 0.0
vllm:num_requests_waiting 0.0
vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc 0.0
vllm:prompt_tokens_total 38.0
vllm:generation_tokens_total 13.0
vllm:request_success_total{...} 1.0
vllm:e2e_request_latency_seconds_bucket{...}These metrics answer operational questions that Ollama cannot:

• num_requests_waiting — is the inference engine keeping up with demand?

• gpu_cache_usage_perc — is KV cache becoming a bottleneck?

• e2e_request_latency_seconds — what is the actual latency distribution?
Adding NIM as a Prometheus scrape target:
scrape_configs:
- job_name: "nim"
static_configs:
- targets: ["localhost:8000"]
metrics_path: "/metrics"Combined with DCGM Exporter — which reports GPU hardware metrics including VRAM usage, temperature, and utilization — this creates two complementary observability layers: what the inference engine is doing, and what the GPU beneath it is doing. When latency increases, the combination tells you whether the cause is GPU saturation, KV cache exhaustion, or something at the application level.

Connecting NIM to LiteLLM
If a LiteLLM gateway already sits in front of Ollama, adding NIM requires one additional block in litellm_config.yaml:
- model_name: llama-3.1-8b-nim
litellm_params:
model: openai/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct
api_base: http://<SERVER_IP>:8000/v1
api_key: dummyNIM’s OpenAI-compatible API is identified by LiteLLM using the openai/ prefix. No application code changes are needed. Every consumer that already targets the LiteLLM gateway begins routing through NIM without any modification.
The result:
Consumer → LiteLLM → NIM (production models, optimized inference)
→ Ollama (experimentation, broad model compatibility)LiteLLM manages both simultaneously. Token accounting, request tracing through Langfuse, and rate limiting apply equally to both backends.

Ollama vs NIM
Ollama NIM Target use case Developer tooling Production inference Inference engine llama.cpp vLLM / TensorRT-LLM Concurrent requests Sequential Continuous batching Model precision Community 4-bit GGUF NVIDIA fp8 / fp16 Prometheus metrics None /metrics endpoint VRAM control Limited Full via pass
